epithets: She of the Eight Thunder Gods
symbols: eight thunder-beasts born from her corpse

description: Also known as Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊邪那美命). Associated with Creation, femininity, birth, the earth, islands. Known as Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊邪那美命) - "She Who Invites". Symbolized by the underworld.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Goddess of Magic & Motherhood | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of the Sky | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of Love & Joy | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings at Funerals
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, offerings are sometimes made at funerals to appease her spirit, particularly if the deceased died during childbirth. These offerings are intended to ensure a peaceful passage to the afterlife and prevent any vengeful actions from the realm of the dead.

worshipPractices.name: Shrine Dedications (Rare)
worshipPractices.description: Though uncommon, some smaller, lesser-known shrines may be dedicated to Izanami, often focusing on her role as a mother goddess and creator. These shrines might feature fertility symbols and are visited by those seeking blessings for childbirth or family well-being.

festivals.name: Obon Festival
festivals.description: Though not directly focused on Izanami, the Obon festival, a Buddhist custom to honor the spirits of ancestors, indirectly acknowledges her role as the source of all life and the ruler of the underworld where many spirits reside.  Families welcome back the spirits of their deceased relatives with offerings and celebrations.

festivals.name: Setsubun
festivals.description: Setsubun, while focused on expelling bad luck and welcoming good fortune for the new year, indirectly relates to Izanami by warding off evil spirits that might escape from the underworld she governs. The ritual of bean-throwing aims to cleanse homes and prevent negative influences.

cultural.modernLegacy: Izanami's story continues to inspire modern Japanese art and literature, often explored in themes of creation, loss, and the complex relationship between life and death. Her narrative provides a foundation for understanding Japanese views on the natural world, the cycle of life, and the interconnectedness of opposing forces, influencing manga, anime, and theatrical productions.

associations.name: Creation
associations.significance: Izanami played a crucial role in the creation of the Japanese islands and many deities.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: Izanami is strongly associated with the physical creation and foundation of the land.

associations.name: Black
associations.significance: Black symbolizes the underworld and death, domains to which Izanami retreated after her death.

associations.name: Femininity
associations.significance: As a female deity, Izanami embodies feminine power and the generative principle.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The moon is often associated with feminine energy, cyclical change, and the hidden realms, mirroring Izanami's connection to life and death.

associations.name: Two
associations.significance: Reflects her pairing with Izanagi in the creation myth, representing duality and partnership.

associations.name: Lotus
associations.significance: Symbolizes rebirth and purity, reflecting the cycle of life and death associated with Izanami.

associations.name: Underworld
associations.significance: Izanami rules the underworld after her death, making it a powerful symbol of her domain.

companions.name: Izanagi
companions.description: Izanagi is Izanami's husband and brother, with whom she created the Japanese islands and many deities. They worked together in the act of creation until Izanami's death.

companions.name: Kagutsuchi
companions.description: Kagutsuchi is the fire deity whose birth caused Izanami's death. While not a companion, his existence is intrinsically linked to Izanami's fate.

companions.name: Shinigami
companions.description: In some interpretations, Izanami commands the Shinigami in Yomi, the land of the dead. They are servants to the queen of the underworld.

companions.name: Yamato Takeru
companions.description: While not directly aligned, Yamato Takeru is a hero who ventured into the underworld and returned, showcasing a defiance of Izanami's domain and indirectly highlighting her power.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Birth of the Islands
extendedContent.content:
Izanami's origins are chronicled in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the primary written records of Japanese mythology. Born from the primordial chaos, she emerged as one of the last of the seven generations of kami (gods) following the creation of Heaven and Earth. She and her brother-husband, Izanagi, were tasked by the celestial deities with solidifying the formless land and bringing order to the world. They were given the Amenonuhoko (heavenly jeweled spear), with which they stirred the primordial ocean. When they lifted the spear, the brine that dripped from it formed the island of Onogoro-shima, the first landmass. 

Izanami and Izanagi descended to this island and erected a palace, the Yahiro-dono. Guided by the divine instructions, they circled a heavenly pillar, Amenomihashira, in opposite directions. Upon meeting, Izanami spoke first, declaring her delight. This led to the birth of their first child, Hiruko (leech-child), who was deformed and cast away. Understanding their mistake, they consulted the other gods, who revealed that Izanami, being female, should not have spoken first during the marriage ritual. 

Rectifying their actions, they repeated the ceremony, with Izanagi speaking first. This time, their union proved fruitful, resulting in the birth of the islands of Japan. This narrative highlights Izanami's crucial role in the creation myth, establishing her as a foundational figure in the Shinto pantheon and connecting her intimately with the geography of Japan.

extendedContent.title: The Birth of Deities and the Descent into Yomi
extendedContent.content:
Following the successful creation of the Japanese archipelago, Izanami and Izanagi continued to procreate, giving birth to a multitude of deities representing various aspects of nature and the world. These included gods of the wind, mountains, trees, and other natural elements. However, Izanami's role as a creator came to a tragic end during the birth of Kagutsuchi, the fire god. The intense heat severely burned her, leading to her death. This event marks a pivotal turning point in the myth, signifying the transition from creation to death and the introduction of mortality into the world.

Consumed by grief and rage, Izanagi slew Kagutsuchi to avenge his wife's death. He then decided to descend into Yomi, the land of the dead, to retrieve Izanami. He found her in the dark and shadowy realm, but she had already consumed the food of the underworld, binding her to that realm. Despite this, Izanami pleaded with Izanagi to not look at her, while she would ask the gods of Yomi to allow her to leave. Izanagi, unable to contain his curiosity and longing, disobeyed her and lit a torch. He was horrified to discover her decaying and grotesque form, swarming with maggots and demons. 

This sight filled Izanagi with terror, and he fled from Yomi. Izanami, enraged by his betrayal and disgust, ordered the Shikome (hideous female spirits) to pursue him. The subsequent chase and Izanagi's escape from Yomi, sealing the entrance with a massive boulder, solidified the separation between the world of the living and the world of the dead. This narrative establishes Izanami as the ruler of Yomi, forever associated with death and the underworld, a stark contrast to her earlier role as the creator of life.

extendedContent.title: Izanami's Iconography and Representation
extendedContent.content:
Unlike some deities with clear and consistent iconography, Izanami's visual representation is relatively limited. There are no established traditional depictions in the same way one might find for Amaterasu or Susanoo. This is partly due to her association with death and the underworld, realms often shrouded in mystery and less frequently depicted in visual art. However, when represented, she is typically portrayed as a female figure, often in traditional Japanese garb. Her expression might convey a sense of solemnity or even sorrow, reflecting her tragic fate and her role as the ruler of Yomi.

Due to the nature of Yomi, symbolic representations are more common than direct depictions of Izanami. Caves and entrances to underground locations can be seen as symbolic representations of Yomi. Shinto shrines dedicated to her are less prevalent compared to other major deities, but some exist, often located in areas with connections to death or natural boundaries. Furthermore, masks, sculptures, and other ritual objects associated with funeral rites and ancestor veneration can be indirectly linked to Izanami, as she is the ultimate ancestor and the ruler of the realm to which the deceased return.

Though not visually prominent in the same way as other deities, Izanami's presence is deeply felt through the cultural understanding of death, the underworld, and the cycle of life. Her lack of a fixed iconography reinforces her position as a powerful but ultimately unknowable figure, residing in a realm beyond human comprehension.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Shrines Dedicated to Izanami
extendedContent.content:
While Izanami is not as widely venerated as some other major Shinto deities like Amaterasu, certain shrines and rituals are dedicated to her, reflecting her profound influence on Japanese beliefs. Izanami-Jingu in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, is considered one of the most important shrines dedicated to her. It is believed to be built near the location where she was buried after her death. The shrine enshrines both Izanami and Izanagi, recognizing their joint role in the creation of Japan and the birth of the kami. Pilgrims visit this shrine to pay respects to Izanami and to seek blessings for childbirth, fertility, and protection from misfortune.

Other smaller shrines and sacred sites dedicated to Izanami can be found throughout Japan, particularly in regions with strong ties to ancient mythology and folklore. These sites often feature natural elements such as caves, springs, or forests, which are considered to be gateways to the spirit world or places where the kami reside. Rituals performed at these shrines often involve offerings of food, sake, and other items, as well as prayers and ceremonies intended to honor Izanami and to seek her favor. 

While direct worship of Izanami may be less common than that of other deities, her presence is felt in rituals related to death, ancestor veneration, and the cycle of life. Beliefs surrounding the afterlife and the spirit world, heavily influenced by the myth of Izanami and Yomi, continue to shape Japanese cultural practices and religious observances.

extendedContent.title: Izanami's Role in Cosmic Order and Duality
extendedContent.content:
Izanami's role extends far beyond mere creation; she embodies a fundamental duality in the cosmic order. Her transition from creator to ruler of the underworld reflects the cyclical nature of life and death, a concept deeply ingrained in Japanese thought. She is not simply a goddess of death, but rather an integral part of the natural progression from birth to decay, and potentially, rebirth. This makes her a necessary, albeit feared, aspect of existence. 

The stark contrast between Izanami's initial role as a life-giver alongside Izanagi and her subsequent transformation into a figure associated with death highlights the inherent tension between creation and destruction, order and chaos. This duality is not seen as inherently negative but rather as a necessary balance within the universe. Izanami's presence in Yomi ensures that the cycle continues, preventing the world from becoming overrun with life and allowing for renewal. She is the necessary counterpart to creation, a reminder of mortality and the impermanence of all things.

Her story also highlights the dangers of disrupting natural boundaries and the consequences of crossing into forbidden realms. Izanagi's violation of the taboo in Yomi and his subsequent escape reinforces the separation between the living and the dead, establishing clear boundaries that must be respected. Izanami, therefore, serves as a guardian of these boundaries, ensuring that the cosmic order is maintained and that the balance between life and death is preserved.
